sentences.en: The Painting Fool is one of a growing number of computer programs which, so their makers claim, possess creative talents.
sentences.zh: “绘画傻瓜”是越来越多电脑程序中的一个；其制作者声称，这些程序拥有创造才能。

grammar.note: which 引导定语从句修饰 programs；so their makers claim 为插入语。

sentences.en: Classical music by an artificial composer has had audiences enraptured, and even tricked them into believing a human was behind the score.
sentences.zh: 由人工作曲家创作的古典音乐曾让观众陶醉，甚至骗过他们，让他们相信乐谱背后是人类。

grammar.type: 并列谓语 + trick into doing
grammar.note: has had 与 tricked 并列；trick somebody into doing 表“诱使某人相信/做”。

sentences.en: Artworks painted by a robot have sold for thousands of dollars and been hung in prestigious galleries.
sentences.zh: 机器人绘制的艺术品已经卖到数千美元，并被挂在知名画廊中。

grammar.note: painted by a robot 修饰 Artworks；have sold 与 been hung 并列。

sentences.en: And software has been built which creates art that could not have been imagined by the programmer.
sentences.zh: 还有人开发出软件，能够创造连程序员本人都无法想象的艺术。

grammar.type: 被动语态 + 双层定语从句
grammar.note: which 修饰 software；that could not... 修饰 art。

sentences.en: Human beings are the only species to perform sophisticated creative acts regularly.
sentences.zh: 人类是唯一经常进行复杂创造行为的物种。

grammar.type: 主系表 + 不定式定语
grammar.note: to perform... 修饰 species。

sentences.en: If we can break this process down into computer code, where does that leave human creativity?
sentences.zh: 如果我们能把这一过程拆解成计算机代码，那人类创造力将置于何地？

grammar.note: If 从句提出假设；where does that leave... 表示对意义/地位的追问。

sentences.en: This is a question at the very core of humanity, says Geraint Wiggins, a computational creativity researcher at Goldsmiths, University of London.
sentences.zh: 伦敦大学金史密斯学院计算创造力研究者 Geraint Wiggins 说，这是一个触及人性核心的问题。

grammar.note: a computational creativity researcher... 是 Geraint Wiggins 的同位语。

sentences.en: It scares a lot of people.
sentences.zh: 它让很多人感到害怕。

grammar.note: It 指代前文机器创造力问题。

sentences.en: They are worried that it is taking something special away from what it means to be human.
sentences.zh: 他们担心，它正在从“成为人类”这件事中夺走某种特殊的东西。

grammar.type: that 宾语从句 + what 从句
grammar.note: worried 后接 that 从句；what it means to be human 作 from 的宾语。

sentences.en: To some extent, we are all familiar with computerised art.
sentences.zh: 在某种程度上，我们都熟悉计算机化艺术。

grammar.note: To some extent 限定程度；be familiar with 表熟悉。

sentences.en: The question is: where does the work of the artist stop and the creativity of the computer begin?
sentences.zh: 问题是：艺术家的工作在哪里停止，计算机的创造力又从哪里开始？

grammar.note: 冒号后用直接疑问句解释 The question。

sentences.en: Consider one of the oldest machine artists, Aaron, a robot that has had paintings exhibited in London's Tate Modern and the San Francisco Museum of Modern Art.
sentences.zh: 以最早的机器艺术家之一 Aaron 为例，它是一个机器人，其画作曾在伦敦泰特现代美术馆和旧金山现代艺术博物馆展出。

grammar.type: 祈使句 + 同位语 + 定语从句
grammar.note: Aaron 是 one of... 的同位语；that 引导定语从句修饰 robot。

sentences.en: Aaron can pick up a paintbrush and paint on canvas on its own.
sentences.zh: Aaron 能自己拿起画笔在画布上作画。

grammar.note: pick up 与 paint 并列；on its own 表独立完成。

sentences.en: Impressive perhaps, but it is still little more than a tool to realise the programmer's own creative ideas.
sentences.zh: 这或许令人印象深刻，但它仍不过是实现程序员自身创意的工具。

grammar.note: Impressive perhaps 省略 it is；little more than 表“不过是”。

sentences.en: Simon Colton, the designer of the Painting Fool, is keen to make sure his creation doesn't attract the same criticism.
sentences.zh: “绘画傻瓜”的设计者 Simon Colton 很想确保他的作品不会招致同样的批评。

grammar.note: the designer... 是同位语；to make sure... 表目的/倾向。

sentences.en: Unlike earlier artists such as Aaron, the Painting Fool only needs minimal direction and can come up with its own concepts by going online for material.
sentences.zh: 与 Aaron 等早期“艺术家”不同，“绘画傻瓜”只需要极少指导，并能通过上网搜集素材提出自己的概念。

grammar.type: Unlike 对比 + 并列谓语
grammar.note: Unlike 引出对比对象；needs 与 can come up with 并列。

sentences.en: The software runs its own web searches and trawls through social media sites.
sentences.zh: 该软件会自行进行网页搜索，并在社交媒体网站中搜罗信息。

grammar.note: runs 与 trawls through 并列。

sentences.en: It is now beginning to display a kind of imagination too, creating pictures from scratch.
sentences.zh: 它现在也开始展现某种想象力，从零开始创作图像。

grammar.type: 现在进行时 + 分词补充
grammar.note: is beginning to display 表正在发展；creating... 作补充说明。

sentences.en: One of its original works is a series of fuzzy landscapes, depicting trees and sky.
sentences.zh: 它的一件原创作品是一系列模糊的风景画，描绘树木和天空。

grammar.note: depicting... 修饰 landscapes。

sentences.en: While some might say they have a mechanical look, Colton argues that such reactions arise from people's double standards towards software-produced and human-produced art.
sentences.zh: 尽管有些人可能说它们看起来机械，Colton 认为这种反应源于人们对软件创作和人类创作艺术的双重标准。

grammar.type: while 让步 + that 宾语从句
grammar.note: While 引导让步；argues 后接 that 从句；arise from 表来源。

sentences.en: After all, he says, consider that the Painting Fool painted the landscapes without referring to a photo.
sentences.zh: 毕竟，他说，要考虑到“绘画傻瓜”在没有参考照片的情况下画出了这些风景。

grammar.note: consider 后接 that 从句；without doing 表“没有……”。

sentences.en: If a child painted a new scene from its head, you'd say it has a certain level of imagination, he points out.
sentences.zh: 他指出，如果一个孩子凭脑海想象画出新场景，你会说他有一定程度的想象力。

grammar.type: if 条件句 + 宾语从句
grammar.note: If 从句设例；you'd say 后接从句。

sentences.en: The same should be true of a machine.
sentences.zh: 同样的判断也应该适用于机器。

grammar.type: 情态动词 + be true of
grammar.note: should 表应然；be true of 表“适用于”。

sentences.en: Software bugs can also lead to unexpected results.
sentences.zh: 软件错误也可能导致意想不到的结果。

grammar.note: can lead to 表可能导致。

sentences.en: Some of the Painting Fool's paintings of a chair came out in black and white, thanks to a technical glitch.
sentences.zh: 由于一个技术故障，“绘画傻瓜”的一些椅子画作呈现为黑白效果。

grammar.note: came out 表结果呈现；thanks to 引出原因。

sentences.en: This gives the work an eerie, ghostlike quality.
sentences.zh: 这赋予作品一种怪异、幽灵般的质感。

grammar.note: gives the work a quality 为 give + 间接宾语 + 直接宾语。

sentences.en: Human artists like the renowned Ellsworth Kelly are lauded for limiting their colour palette - so why should computers be any different?
sentences.zh: 像著名的 Ellsworth Kelly 这样的人类艺术家会因限制调色板而受到赞扬，那么为什么计算机就该不同呢？

grammar.note: are lauded for 表因……受赞扬；why should... 反问双重标准。

sentences.en: Researchers like Colton don't believe it is right to measure machine creativity directly to that of humans who have had millennia to develop our skills.
sentences.zh: 像 Colton 这样的研究者认为，直接把机器创造力与人类创造力衡量比较并不公平，因为人类已有数千年发展技能。

grammar.note: believe 后省略 that；it is right to... 中 it 为形式主语；that of humans 指代 creativity。

sentences.en: Others, though, are fascinated by the prospect that a computer might create something as original and subtle as our best artists.
sentences.zh: 然而，其他人则着迷于这样一种可能性：计算机也许能创造出与顶尖艺术家一样原创而微妙的东西。

grammar.note: that 从句解释 prospect；as original and subtle as 构成同级比较。

sentences.en: So far, only one has come close.
sentences.zh: 到目前为止，只有一个程序接近做到这一点。

grammar.note: has come close 表“接近成功”。

sentences.en: Composer David Cope invented a program called Experiments in Musical Intelligence, or EMI.
sentences.zh: 作曲家 David Cope 发明了一个名为“音乐智能实验”的程序，简称 EMI。

grammar.note: called... 修饰 program；or EMI 给出简称。

sentences.en: Not only did EMI create compositions in Cope's style, but also that of the most revered classical composers, including Bach, Chopin and Mozart.
sentences.zh: EMI 不仅能创作 Cope 风格的作品，还能创作最受尊崇的古典作曲家风格的作品，包括巴赫、肖邦和莫扎特。

grammar.type: not only 倒装 + but also
grammar.note: Not only 位于句首引起助动词 did 倒装；that 指代 style。

sentences.en: Audiences were moved to tears, and EMI even fooled classical music experts into thinking they were hearing genuine Bach.
sentences.zh: 观众被感动落泪，EMI 甚至骗过古典音乐专家，让他们以为自己听到的是真正的巴赫。

grammar.note: were moved to tears 为被动；fooled...into thinking 表使误以为。

sentences.en: Not everyone was impressed however.
sentences.zh: 然而，并非所有人都对此印象深刻。

grammar.note: Not everyone 表“并非每个人”。

sentences.en: Some, such as Wiggins, have blasted Cope's work as pseudoscience, and condemned him for his deliberately vague explanation of how the software worked.
sentences.zh: 一些人，如 Wiggins，猛烈批评 Cope 的作品是伪科学，并谴责他故意含糊解释软件如何运作。

grammar.type: 并列谓语 + how 从句
grammar.note: have blasted 与 condemned 并列；how the software worked 作 explanation 的内容。

sentences.en: Meanwhile, Douglas Hofstadter of Indiana University said EMI created replicas which still rely completely on the original artist's creative impulses.
sentences.zh: 与此同时，印第安纳大学的 Douglas Hofstadter 说，EMI 创造的是复制品，仍然完全依赖原艺术家的创作冲动。

grammar.type: 宾语从句 + 定语从句
grammar.note: said 后接宾语从句；which 修饰 replicas。

sentences.en: When audiences found out the truth they were often outraged with Cope, and one music lover even tried to punch him.
sentences.zh: 当观众发现真相后，他们常常对 Cope 感到愤怒，甚至有一位音乐爱好者试图打他。

grammar.type: when 时间从句 + 并列句
grammar.note: When 从句说明时间；were outraged 与 tried 并列描述反应。

sentences.en: Amid such controversy, Cope destroyed EMI's vital databases.
sentences.zh: 在这样的争议中，Cope 销毁了 EMI 的关键数据库。

grammar.note: Amid such controversy 表背景；destroyed 为主谓宾。

sentences.en: But why did so many people love the music, yet recoil when they discovered how it was composed?
sentences.zh: 但是，为什么那么多人喜欢这些音乐，却在发现其创作方式后退缩反感呢？

grammar.type: 疑问句 + when 时间从句
grammar.note: love 与 recoil 形成转折；how it was composed 作 discovered 的宾语。

sentences.en: A study by computer scientist David Moffat of Glasgow Caledonian University provides a clue.
sentences.zh: 格拉斯哥卡利多尼亚大学计算机科学家 David Moffat 的一项研究提供了线索。

grammar.note: by... 修饰 study；provides a clue 为谓语宾语。

sentences.en: He asked both expert musicians and non-experts to assess six compositions.
sentences.zh: 他要求专业音乐家和非专业人士评估六首作品。

grammar.note: both...and... 连接两个宾语；to assess 为宾补。

sentences.en: The participants weren't told beforehand whether the tunes were composed by humans or computers, but were asked to guess, and then rate how much they liked each one.
sentences.zh: 参与者事先没有被告知这些曲子是人类还是计算机创作的，而是被要求猜测，然后评价自己有多喜欢每一首。

grammar.type: 被动语态 + whether 从句
grammar.note: weren't told 与 were asked 并列被动；whether 从句作 told 的内容。

sentences.en: People who thought the composer was a computer tended to dislike the piece more than those who believed it was human.
sentences.zh: 认为作曲者是计算机的人，往往比认为作曲者是人类的人更不喜欢该作品。

grammar.note: 两个 who 从句分别修饰 People 与 those；more than 构成比较。

sentences.en: This was true even among the experts, who might have been expected to be more objective in their analyses.
sentences.zh: 即使在本应更客观分析的专家中，这一点也成立。

grammar.note: who 补充说明 experts；might have been expected 表本可期待。

sentences.en: Where does this prejudice come from?
sentences.zh: 这种偏见来自哪里？

grammar.note: come from 询问来源。

sentences.en: Paul Bloom of Yale University has a suggestion: he reckons part of the pleasure we get from art stems from the creative process behind the work.
sentences.zh: 耶鲁大学的 Paul Bloom 有一个解释：他认为，我们从艺术中获得的部分愉悦源于作品背后的创作过程。

grammar.type: 冒号解释 + 宾语从句
grammar.note: 冒号后解释 suggestion；we get from art 修饰 pleasure；stems from 表来源。

sentences.en: This can give it an irresistible essence, says Bloom.
sentences.zh: Bloom 说，这能赋予它一种不可抗拒的本质。

grammar.note: give it an essence 为 give 双宾结构。

sentences.en: Meanwhile, experiments by Justin Kruger of New York University have shown that people's enjoyment of an artwork increases if they think more time and effort was needed to create it.
sentences.zh: 与此同时，纽约大学 Justin Kruger 的实验表明，如果人们认为创作某件艺术品需要更多时间和努力，他们对它的欣赏会增加。

grammar.type: that 宾语从句 + if 条件句
grammar.note: have shown 后接 that 从句；if 引导条件；was needed 为被动。

sentences.en: Similarly, Colton thinks that when people experience art, they wonder what the artist might have been thinking or what the artist is trying to tell them.
sentences.zh: 同样，Colton 认为，当人们体验艺术时，他们会想艺术家当时可能在想什么，或试图告诉他们什么。

grammar.type: that 宾语从句 + when 从句
grammar.note: thinks 后接 that 从句；when 引导时间；两个 what 从句作 wonder 的宾语。

sentences.en: It seems obvious, therefore, that with computers producing art, this speculation is cut short - there's nothing to explore.
sentences.zh: 因此，显而易见，当计算机创作艺术时，这种揣测被截断了——没有什么可探索的。

grammar.type: it 形式主语 + with 复合结构
grammar.note: It seems obvious that... 中 it 为形式主语；with computers producing art 表背景。

sentences.en: But as technology becomes increasingly complex, finding those greater depths in computer art could become possible.
sentences.zh: 但随着技术变得日益复杂，在计算机艺术中发现更深层次的内容可能会成为可能。

grammar.type: as 时间/伴随从句 + 动名词主语
grammar.note: as 表随着；finding... 作主语；could become possible 表可能性。

sentences.en: This is precisely why Colton asks the Painting Fool to tap into online social networks for its inspiration: hopefully this way it will choose themes that will already be meaningful to us.
sentences.zh: 这正是 Colton 要求“绘画傻瓜”利用在线社交网络获取灵感的原因：希望通过这种方式，它会选择对我们来说已经有意义的主题。

grammar.type: why 表语从句 + 冒号解释
grammar.note: why 引导表语从句；ask...to do；冒号后解释目的。

questions.title: Questions 27-31 · Multiple choice
instructions: Choose the correct letter, A, B, C or D.

items.prompt: What is the writer suggesting about computer-produced works in the first paragraph?
According to Geraint Wiggins, why are many people worried by computer art?
What is a key difference between Aaron and the Painting Fool?
What point does Simon Colton make in the fourth paragraph?
The writer refers to the paintings of a chair as an example of computer art which...

questions.title: Questions 32-37 · Matching sentence endings
instructions: Complete each sentence with the correct ending, A-G.
A generating work that was virtually indistinguishable from that of humans.
B knowing whether it was the work of humans or software.
C producing work entirely dependent on the imagination of its creator.
D comparing the artistic achievements of humans and computers.
E revealing the technical details of his program.
F persuading the public to appreciate computer art.
G discovering that it was the product of a computer program.

items.prompt: Simon Colton says it is important to consider the long-term view when...
David Cope's EMI software surprised people by...
Geraint Wiggins criticised Cope for not...
Douglas Hofstadter claimed that EMI was...
Audiences who had listened to EMI's music became angry after...
The participants in David Moffat's study had to assess music without...

questions.title: Questions 38-40 · YES / NO / NOT GIVEN
instructions: Do the following statements agree with the claims of the writer?

items.prompt: Moffat's research may help explain people's reactions to EMI.
The non-experts in Moffat's study all responded in a predictable way.
Justin Kruger's findings cast doubt on Paul Bloom's theory about people's prejudice towards computer art.
